critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

This oncology survival model paper reports C-index = 0.79 with external validation and claims the model is suitable for triaging referrals to a capacity-constrained supportive-care program.

The primary methodological problem is a metric mismatch. The concordance index measures global pairwise survival discrimination — it summarizes how well the model orders all patient pairs by predicted event time. The referral triage question is different: which specific 12% of active patients should receive immediate program slots? These are different statistical questions. A model with good global concordance can have poor precision at the specific 12th-percentile threshold that matters for referral decisions.

The paper omits: (1) PPV at the 12% referral budget — how many flagged patients are among those who would most benefit; (2) decision-curve net benefit analysis — whether model-guided referrals produce better outcomes than other allocation strategies given the cost of false positives; (3) lead-time evaluation — whether patients are identified early enough for supportive care to produce clinical benefit before functional decline or death; (4) comparison to the existing clinical heuristic (unplanned admissions + ECOG decline) — without this, the incremental value of the model over current practice is unknown.

The external validation at a second center with similar concordance is a genuine scientific strength. Routine EHR feature availability is also a real operational advantage. These properties establish the model as a generalizable survival ranker. They do not establish that the model improves referral triage under the 12% slot constraint.

The paper's assertion that 'higher concordance necessarily implies better triage' is not logically valid. This claim requires additional support from capacity-specific evaluation metrics.

Verdict: critique_wins.
